metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC2/AC5) with a proving command that asserts raw_verdicts.jsonl row completeness before analysis — e.g., verify every sample ticket_id appears with replay_index=1, and every FP-pool ticket_id appears with all three replay_index values — so incomplete live runs are caught before offline summaries are computed.
evidence: 'Completion assertion before any summary: every one of the ~200 sample tickets has a replay_index=1 row, and every FP-pool ticket has rows for all of replay_index ∈ {1,2,3}'
This assertion is prose-only; no AC proving command verifies raw_verdicts.jsonl row counts before the analyze step runs.
findings.finding: The prose completion assertion — 'every one of the ~200 sample tickets has a replay_index=1 row, and every FP-pool ticket has rows for all of replay_index ∈ {1,2,3}' — appears only in the body text with no corresponding AC proving command. All downstream offline ACs (AC2–AC5) re-derive summaries from raw_verdicts.jsonl, so if that file is incomplete (e.g., the live run was interrupted), the analysis silently runs over partial data and the ACs still pass.
findings.location: Scope / 'The replay (candidate side)' section; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers this)
scenarios: Live LLM run is interrupted after 150 tickets; executor commits the partial raw_verdicts.jsonl; all offline ACs (AC2–AC5) pass on the partial data, producing a silent underpowered result.
FP-pool replay terminates after replay_index=1 only; the two-level-median metrics in fp_control.json are computed over 1 replay instead of 3, but no AC catches this.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 (or as a new AC before AC2): `python docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/replay_candidate.py run --candidate <label> --verify-complete` (or an equivalent jq/awk one-liner such as: `jq -r 'select(.replay_index==1)|.ticket_id' runs/e3/<label>/raw_verdicts.jsonl | sort > /tmp/cand_ids.txt && jq -r '.ticket_id' runs/e3/sample.jsonl | sort > /tmp/sample_ids.txt && diff /tmp/sample_ids.txt /tmp/cand_ids.txt` plus an analogous check for the FP-pool replay_index ∈ {1,2,3}).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For the FP-control pass/fail decision in def 7, use `sidecar.fix_unit_key` (criteria-free location+text fingerprint) rather than `norm_id` as the deduplication key when computing `new_blocking_median` and `new_advisory_median_delta`, reserving `norm_id` for the per-criterion deltas in def 6 where criterion identity IS meaningful.
evidence: "norm_id is... COMPUTED — verdict blocking[]/advisory[] findings carry no norm_id key... the driver imports from rebar.llm.plan_review import sidecar and computes sidecar.norm_id(f) for every finding on BOTH sides uniformly"
sidecar.py:268: `basis = " ".join(tokens) + "|" + ",".join(sorted(finding.get("criteria", []) or []))` — criteria are part of the hash
sidecar.py:279-290: `fix_unit_key` is explicitly documented as 'CRITERIA-FREE' for exactly this case: 'norm_id cannot serve here — it bakes the sorted criteria list into its hash'
R1 changes the G6/E4 rubric (SOTA §6/R1: 'extend G6/E4 rubrics'), which will cause criteria fields on the same defect to differ between candidate and current runs
findings.finding: The plan exclusively uses `sidecar.norm_id` for candidate-vs-current finding matching in def 7 (both new-blocking and new-advisory detection), but `norm_id` bakes the sorted `criteria` list into its hash (`sidecar.py:268`). For R1, which extends the G6/E4 rubric by adding or restructuring criteria, a finding with identical text but altered criteria assignment will mint a different `norm_id`, making it appear as a NEW blocking or advisory finding even though the underlying defect is the same one the current gate already surfaces. This directly inflates `new_blocking_median` and `new_advisory_median_delta`, risking a false R1 FAIL on a sound rubric change. The codebase already provides `sidecar.fix_unit_key` (criteria-free, location+finding-text fingerprint, `sidecar.py:279-290`) precisely for cases where criterion identity must not affect deduplication.
findings.location: def 7 — LLM nondeterminism control (precise tier); also def 6 criterion_deltas schema
scenarios: R1 rubric extension adds a criterion tag to an existing finding category; candidate run emits that finding with criteria=["G6","R1_probe"] while current run emits criteria=["G6"]; norm_id differs → counted as new blocking finding → new_blocking_median inflated → R1 may falsely fail a good rubric change
Any rubric change that reassigns or adds criteria IDs to existing finding patterns will trigger the same inflation
findings.suggested_fix: In `e3_metrics.fp_control_eval`, replace `sidecar.norm_id(f)` with `sidecar.fix_unit_key(f)` in the set-difference computations for `new_adv_i(t)` and `blocked_i(t)` (the candidate-vs-current finding comparisons). Keep `norm_id` for `criterion_deltas` (def 6) where per-criterion granularity is the point. Update the golden tests in `test_e3_metrics.py` to exercise a scenario where a finding keeps identical text but changes criteria, confirming it is NOT counted as new under fix_unit_key.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `observed_total` (int: total rows entering the matrix for this segment, equal to the sum of all four cells plus `excluded.total`) to the named schema of `transition_matrix.json` in the 'Verdict → matrix mapping' section.
evidence: AC2 jq: `(.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block + .cells.block_to_pass + .cells.block_to_block + .excluded.total) == .observed_total`
Plan schema definition in 'Verdict → matrix mapping': names `cells`, `excluded.total`, `flip_rate`, `segments[]` but never names the field `observed_total`
ABSENCE: no line in the plan defining `observed_total` as a required key in `transition_matrix.json`
findings.finding: The `transition_matrix.json` schema used in AC2's jq proving command references a field named `.observed_total`, but this field is never defined by name in the plan's 'Verdict → matrix mapping' section (def 2 / the matrix schema). The plan describes the consistency invariant as `cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block + cells.block_to_pass + cells.block_to_block + excluded.total` equalling the row total, but the field name an executor must emit to satisfy the AC2 jq (`(.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block + .cells.block_to_pass + .cells.block_to_block + .excluded.total) == .observed_total`) is never declared in the schema definition.
findings.location: Def 2 / 'Verdict → matrix mapping' section and AC2 jq proving command
scenarios: An executor implementing `build_matrix` in e3_metrics.py would not know to emit `observed_total`, causing AC2's jq to return `null == null` (false) or a jq error depending on version, silently failing the consistency check.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Verdict → matrix mapping' definition, add: `observed_total` (int) = the count of tickets processed for this segment (= `cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block + cells.block_to_pass + cells.block_to_block + excluded.total`). This makes the AC2 jq provably executable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) with a jq shape-check on `runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json` verifying it has at least one row and carries the same 10 keys as `criterion_deltas.jsonl` rows (or the documented per-criterion aggregated schema).
evidence: Scope: 'A rollup `criterion_deltas_summary.json` aggregates the same fields per criterion.'
AC3 jq command targets `criterion_deltas.jsonl` only; no AC references `criterion_deltas_summary.json`
ABSENCE: no jq or pytest command anywhere in ACs names `criterion_deltas_summary.json`
findings.finding: `criterion_deltas_summary.json` is a named deliverable in the Scope section ('A rollup `criterion_deltas_summary.json` aggregates the same fields per criterion') but no acceptance criterion verifies its existence, schema, or correctness. Every other output file in Scope has a covering AC; this one does not.
findings.location: Scope section ('criterion_deltas_summary.json') vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC3 covers only criterion_deltas.jsonl)
scenarios: The summary file is delivered with a schema bug or missing fields and no AC catches it.
An implementer omits the file entirely; all ACs still pass.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: `jq -se 'length>0 and all(.[]; has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta"))' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json`

findings.finding: `criterion_deltas_summary.json` is a named deliverable (described in def 6 as 'A rollup criterion_deltas_summary.json aggregates the same fields per criterion' and listed in the Scope file inventory under `<candidate>/`) but no AC verifies its existence, schema, or content.
findings.location: What § def 6 ('criterion_deltas_summary.json'); Scope file inventory; Acceptance Criteria (AC3 covers only `criterion_deltas.jsonl` and the golden test)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 with a jq one-liner: `jq -e 'has("criterion") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json` (schema check) and a pytest golden that asserts the rollup aggregates correctly from the per-ticket rows.

findings.finding: The plan describes `criterion_deltas_summary.json` as a distinct committed artifact in both Scope ('per-candidate <candidate>/{…criterion_deltas_summary.json}') and def 6 ('A rollup criterion_deltas_summary.json aggregates the same fields per criterion'), but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this file's existence, schema, or correctness — it is an uncovered deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section (criterion_deltas_summary.json) and def 6; all 7 ACs
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 to add: `jq -e 'has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("net_blocking_delta")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas_summary.json` and add a `pytest tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py -k summary -q` golden test covering the rollup aggregation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the atomicity mechanism for raw_verdicts.jsonl appends (e.g., O_APPEND or file lock) and add an explicit idempotency guard — either a pre-append existence check for (ticket_id, replay_index) or a post-resume deduplication step — so checkpoint/resume cannot produce duplicate rows.
evidence: 'to runs/e3/<candidate>/raw_verdicts.jsonl, each row keyed by (ticket_id, replay_index), atomic append per row (checkpoint/resume)'
No specification of the atomicity primitive (O_APPEND, flock, etc.)
No specification of a pre-append existence check or post-resume dedup step
The analyze step reads raw_verdicts.jsonl without a stated dedup pass
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'atomic append per row (checkpoint/resume)' for raw_verdicts.jsonl but does not specify the atomicity mechanism (O_APPEND, file lock, etc.), nor does it specify how the driver avoids duplicate (ticket_id, replay_index) rows when a run is resumed after interruption. Without an explicit deduplication guard or idempotency check, a resumed run will append already-completed rows a second time, silently corrupting the JSONL input to the analyze step.
findings.location: What section — 'The replay (candidate side)' paragraph; Scope — harnesses/replay_candidate.py
scenarios: Operator's batch harness is interrupted mid-run and resumed; duplicate rows for completed tickets appear in raw_verdicts.jsonl; fp_control_eval computes metrics over doubled data, yielding incorrect pairing counts and wrong r1_pass decision.

Two parallel replay_candidate.py invocations (e.g., main and candidate runs launched concurrently) both append to their respective files; partial-line interleave corrupts JSONL if O_APPEND is not used.
findings.suggested_fix: Use Python's O_APPEND open mode for line atomicity on POSIX. Before appending each row, check whether a row with the same (ticket_id, replay_index) already exists in the file (load existing keys at driver startup into a set); skip if already present. Document this in the driver spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC7's grep chain to anchor the two remaining required topics, e.g. add `&& grep -q 'two-tier' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md && grep -q 'r1_pass' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md`.
evidence: Scope: 'an E3 section documenting the baseline source + chosen round, cleanly-closed filter, recall-strip + fresh-both-gates design, material-fingerprint drift control, the two-tier / nondeterminism / cost story, and the R1 verdict'
AC7 grep: `grep -q "recall" ... && grep -q "material_fingerprint" ... && grep -q "cleanly-closed" ... && grep -q "all_review_results"`
'two-tier', 'cost', 'nondeterminism', 'r1_pass' / 'R1 verdict' absent from grep anchors
findings.finding: AC7's README proving command spot-checks only four keywords (`recall`, `material_fingerprint`, `cleanly-closed`, `all_review_results`) but the Scope section requires the README to cover six distinct topics: baseline source + chosen round, cleanly-closed filter, recall-strip + fresh-both-gates design, material-fingerprint drift control, two-tier LLM-nondeterminism/cost story, and the R1 verdict. Two of the six required topics ('two-tier cost story' and 'R1 verdict') have no grep anchor in the proving command, so AC7 can be satisfied by a README that omits them.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC7 (README documentation)
scenarios: A README that covers recall-strip and material_fingerprint but omits the R1 verdict and cost story passes AC7 trivially.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the AC7 proving command: `&& grep -q 'two-tier' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md && grep -q 'r1_pass' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `analyze --candidate <label> --verify` exits non-zero (and prints a human-readable diff) when the recomputed `transition_matrix.json` does not match the committed file, mirroring the explicit `exits non-zero unless byte-identical` contract of `sample --verify`.
evidence: AC1: `sample --verify` described as '(offline; re-derives sample + baseline, exits non-zero unless byte-identical to committed)'

AC2: `analyze --candidate <label> --verify` described as '(offline; recomputes `transition_matrix.json` from committed `raw_verdicts.jsonl` + `baseline.jsonl`, asserts equality)' — no exit-code contract stated
ABSENCE: no statement that `analyze --verify` exits non-zero on inequality, or what it prints when assertion fails
findings.finding: The `replay_candidate.py analyze --candidate <label> --verify` sub-command's exit-code / failure behavior on assertion failure is never specified. The `sample --verify` command is explicitly stated to 'exit non-zero unless byte-identical to committed', but `analyze --verify` only says it 'recomputes `transition_matrix.json` from committed `raw_verdicts.jsonl` + `baseline.jsonl`, asserts equality' — 'asserts equality' is ambiguous about the output contract (exit code, error message, diff output).
findings.location: AC2 proving command description
scenarios: An implementor could make `analyze --verify` print a diff and exit 0, or raise an exception, or exit 1 with no output. The AC command `python ... analyze --candidate <label> --verify` would pass CI even if it exits 0 on a mismatch.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC2 description: '(offline; recomputes `transition_matrix.json` from committed `raw_verdicts.jsonl` + `baseline.jsonl`, exits non-zero and prints a diff unless byte-identical to committed)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the fp_control_valid=false recovery procedure in the plan: specify the new pool size formula (e.g., ceil(30 / P_usable) with updated P_usable estimate), the CLI command to re-draw and commit an enlarged fp_control_pool.jsonl, and how the frozen baseline.jsonl is updated for the new pool members.
evidence: 'a run is INVALID only if even the 50-pool cannot yield 30 fully-usable tickets (usable_count < 30), in which case it is re-run after enlarging/re-freezing the pool and yields no R1 verdict'
No CLI subcommand or flag documented for pool enlargement
No specification of the new pool size (e.g., 60? 70?) or the seed adjustment needed to draw a larger sample
fp_control_pool.jsonl is described as a committed artifact with no update procedure
findings.finding: The plan names the `fp_control_valid=false` invalidity outcome ('re-run after enlarging/re-freezing the pool') but provides no operator-facing procedure: no CLI command, no specification of how to enlarge the pool size, and no guidance on how the frozen fp_control_pool.jsonl is updated. This is a non-happy-path exit with a named recovery action but no actionable steps.
findings.location: What — Definition 8 'Over-sampling ticket-drop rule + FP-control validity guard'; AC5
scenarios: Higher-than-expected INDETERMINATE rate (e.g., LLM outage during run) causes usable_count < 30; operator needs to enlarge pool but has no documented command or size target to use.
Operator manually edits fp_control_pool.jsonl incorrectly, breaking the deterministic sampling invariant and making the gate result non-reproducible.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `replay_candidate.py enlarge-pool --new-size N` subcommand (or equivalent) that re-draws the pool at the new size using the same deterministic sampler (Random(1729) over the eligible PASS candidates), commits the updated fp_control_pool.jsonl, and re-freezes baseline rows for the new members. Document the recommended new size as ceil(30 / 0.61 * 1.5) = 74 as a conservative buffer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the sub-floor collapse rule for the case where BOTH verdict cells for a ticket_type are sub-floor: specify whether both collapse into a single unsegmented pool for that ticket_type, or the entire ticket_type is dropped from stratification.
evidence: Plan: 'proportional to the pool with a floor of 5 per non-empty cell (sub-floor cells collapse into the same ticket_type's larger verdict cell)'
ABSENCE: no rule for when both PASS and BLOCK cells for a ticket_type are < 5 (e.g., a rare ticket_type with only 3 PASS and 2 BLOCK tickets in the corpus)
findings.finding: The stratified sampling 'sub-floor cell collapse' rule is structurally incomplete: the plan states 'sub-floor cells collapse into the same ticket_type's larger verdict cell' but does not define which verdict cell is 'larger' when BOTH PASS and BLOCK cells for a given ticket_type are sub-floor (count < 5). In that case, the collapse target is undefined, leaving the driver with no algorithm to follow.
findings.location: 'Stratified sampling frame (reproducible)' section
scenarios: A ticket_type like 'bug' or 'code_review' may have few cleanly-closed tickets with usable plan-v3 baseline rounds. If both PASS (e.g., 3 tickets) and BLOCK (e.g., 2 tickets) cells are sub-floor, collapsing into 'the larger verdict cell' means collapsing 2 into the cell of size 3, but the result (5 total) is still uncertain about the sampling allocation and the driver has no deterministic algorithm.

evidence: description is 24442 chars (> 24000)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State an explicit lower bound or fallback: either assert `|PASS-baseline in 200-sample| ≥ 50` (with a note that the 628-row corpus makes this safe) or specify that if the pool is undersized, the 200-sample is enlarged until 50 PASS-baseline tickets are available.
evidence: 'FP-control pool = deterministic random.Random(1729) draw of 50 from the baseline_verdict == PASS members of the 200-sample (a genuine subset; matches SOTA)'

The 200-sample is stratified by (ticket_type × baseline_verdict) proportional with a floor of 5 per non-empty cell — the exact PASS count in the 200 depends on the corpus split and is not bounded below by 50 in the plan text.
findings.finding: The FP-control pool is drawn as `random.Random(1729).sample(population, 50)` from `baseline_verdict == PASS` members of the 200-sample, but the plan does not assert or guarantee that the 200-sample contains at least 50 PASS-baseline tickets. If the proportional stratified draw yields fewer than 50 PASS tickets (e.g., in a skewed corpus or with a small floor-cell collapse), the `sample(..., 50)` call raises a ValueError and the driver has no stated fallback.
findings.location: Stratified sampling frame section; 'FP-control pool = deterministic random.Random(1729) draw of 50 from the baseline_verdict == PASS members of the 200-sample'
scenarios: A corpus with a high BLOCK rate yields fewer than 50 PASS-baseline tickets in the 200-sample; the driver crashes at pool-draw time with no recovery path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'The 628-row corpus yields ≥N PASS-baseline tickets in the 200-sample (verified offline); if |PASS-baseline| < 50, enlarge the total sample proportionally until the pool reaches 50 before freezing.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the R1 blocking-flip bar across sections: either update 'Why' to state the bar as `max(3, null_floor_pass_to_block)` (not simply '>3/30') and explain why the noise-floor override is safe, or add a cap (e.g., `min(null_floor, 5)`) so the effective bar cannot silently exceed the SOTA-documented threshold.
evidence: Why: 'R1 FAILs if, on 30 sampled historical plans that PASSed and closed with zero post-claim edits, >3/30 (10%) gain a new blocking finding'
def 7: 'R1 FAILs on blocking only if `new_blocking_median > max(3, null_floor_pass_to_block)`'
"the candidate's blocking churn must exceed BOTH the SOTA §5/R1 absolute bar (3/30) AND the gate's own noise floor" — this design rationale appears only in def 7, not in Why
findings.finding: The 'Why' section states the R1 FAIL threshold as '>3/30 (10%) gain a new blocking finding' (citing SOTA doc §5/R1 as the authoritative bar), but def 7 implements the condition as `new_blocking_median > max(3, null_floor_pass_to_block)`. When `null_floor_pass_to_block > 3` (plausible at high LLM nondeterminism), the effective FAIL threshold rises above the SOTA-documented 3 — a candidate producing 4 blocking flips would PASS R1 if the noise floor is 4 or 5. The 'Why' section never acknowledges this deviation, creating a cross-section contradiction: the gate description promises one bar, the implementation spec delivers a (silently) weaker one under noisy conditions.
findings.location: Why section (R1 gate bar description) vs. What / def 7 (r1_pass formula)
scenarios: A reviewer reading 'Why' believes >3 blocking flips always fails R1; the implementation silently allows 4+ flips to pass when noise floor ≥ 4, meaning a genuinely harmful R1 candidate could ship.
An auditor comparing the plan against the SOTA doc sees a discrepancy in the gate bar without explanation.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Why' section, replace the bare '>3/30' description with: 'R1 FAILs if `new_blocking_median > max(3, null_floor_pass_to_block)` — the SOTA absolute bar of 3/30 is the floor; the noise floor overrides it only upward, and def 7 records both values so reviewers can see when the noise floor is the binding constraint.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify rate-limit handling (retry-with-backoff on 429/503) and state whether calls are serialized or parallelized in `replay_candidate.py`, and confirm checkpoint/resume applies to `runs/e3/main/raw_verdicts.jsonl` as well as the candidate file.
evidence: 'Live LLM calls are billable and run once per candidate/main by the executor' — no concurrency or rate-limit strategy stated
'harnesses/run_review_*.py is the batch-driver precedent' — referenced but its concurrency/rate-limit behavior is not inherited or cited
~200 coarse + 50×3 candidate + 50×3 current = ~500 full pipeline calls with no throughput bound or retry-on-429 specification
findings.finding: No rate-limiting, concurrency control, or wall-clock time estimate is specified for the ~500 full `review_plan` LLM batch calls. The driver references `harnesses/run_review_*.py` as precedent but does not state whether calls are serialized, parallelized, or subject to any rate-limit backoff. A naive serial implementation of ~500 pipeline calls without rate-limit handling risks API throttling errors that abort the batch without checkpoint recovery.
findings.location: Scope: `harnesses/replay_candidate.py`; What: 'Replication is TIERED'; The replay (candidate side)
scenarios: API rate limit hit mid-batch causes non-checkpoint-recoverable failure on main/raw_verdicts.jsonl (checkpoint/resume not stated for main runs)
Serial execution of 500 calls takes many hours, increasing the window for transient LLM degradation to corrupt the usable-ticket pool
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `--concurrency` flag (default 1 for safety) and a retry-with-exponential-backoff wrapper around each `review_plan` call; confirm the atomic-append checkpoint pattern is applied identically to `main/raw_verdicts.jsonl`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-flight assertion in `replay_candidate.py` that verifies `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` is honored (e.g., by checking a test-write to the clone is not propagated to the real store) before issuing any `review_plan` call, and fail loud if the guard cannot be confirmed.
evidence: 'With `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`=clone (wins verbatim — config.py:116) and `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`, run `review_plan(...)`' — REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off is set but not verified
No AC or driver step asserts `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` is respected before issuing any LLM call
'The real .tickets-tracker branch/origin are untouched' — asserted as a property but not enforced by the driver
findings.finding: The clone isolation relies on `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` to prevent sidecar writes from reaching the real tracker, but the plan specifies no guard or assertion that this env var is actually honored before the batch begins. If the env var is missing or the sync mechanism ignores it, all ~500 fresh gate runs could write REVIEW_RESULT files to the real `.tickets-tracker`, corrupting the production sidecar history.
findings.location: What: Recall-stripped clone (def 3); 'REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off'
scenarios: Operator runs the harness in an environment where REBAR_SYNC_PUSH is not respected (e.g., a different config.py version), silently writing 500 REVIEW_RESULT entries to production sidecar
A future refactor of config.py changes env-var precedence (config.py:116) without updating this harness, causing silent real-store contamination
findings.suggested_fix: Before the batch loop, perform a canary write to the clone tracker and assert the real `.tickets-tracker` is unmodified; abort with a clear error if the isolation is not verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that adding `tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py` (CI-collected pytest tests for an experiment harness) is authorized by the parent epic (6982); if the parent's scope for experiment stories is only a `docs/experiments/` writeup, either move the tests to `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/` as non-CI scripts or update the parent epic to explicitly include test-suite artifacts for E-experiment harnesses.
evidence: Parent epic (6982) scopes experiment children as: 'each experiment child (E1, E2, E3, E5, E6) is closed with its results committed as a docs/experiments/ writeup (the repo artifact is the criterion; ticket comments mirror it)' — no mention of tests/unit/.
Parent says: 'Calibration assets in docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/ are extended, not replaced.' — the canonical home for experiment artifacts is docs/experiments/, not tests/.
Parent's implementation-target section ('src/rebar/llm/plan_review/…') scopes tests/ changes only implicitly for R-stories (which ship code), not E-experiment stories.
Parent file_impact is empty — no test-suite changes are anticipated at the epic level for experiment stories.
E3 itself notes 'CI-collectable golden tests' confirming these run in the main CI suite, not as ad-hoc experiment scripts.
findings.finding: E3's Scope adds `tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py` — CI-collected pytest tests — which extends the leaf's footprint into the main test suite, a scope the parent epic does not contain for any experiment story.
findings.location: Scope section: '`tests/unit/test_e3_metrics.py`: CI-collectable golden tests for the pure `e3_metrics` functions'
scenarios: The CI test suite now includes permanent, versioned tests for an experiment harness (e3_metrics.py) that lives under docs/ rather than src/. If the harness is later revised or retired, the tests/unit/ files become orphaned maintenance burden.

Another reviewer or implementer of a sibling experiment story (E2, E5, E6) may not realize experiment stories are expected to add to tests/unit/ and will under-scope their own deliverables, creating inconsistency across the E-story set.

findings.finding: The AC3 proving command applies `jq -e 'has("ticket_id") and ... (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")'` directly to `criterion_deltas.jsonl` — a multi-line JSONL file — without `--slurp` or explicit line-by-line iteration. With standard `jq`, processing a JSONL file without `--slurp` evaluates each line's result independently and exits 0 if the final output is truthy, meaning schema violations on all lines except the last are silently ignored. A `criterion_deltas` implementation that outputs a valid first/last row but malformed intermediate rows passes the AC check.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (criterion_deltas proving command): `jq -e 'has("ticket_id") and ... (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas.jsonl`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single `jq -e '...' criterion_deltas.jsonl` with: `jq -se 'map(has("ticket_id") and has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("baseline_blocking_count") and has("candidate_blocking_count") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta") and (.new_norm_ids|type=="array") and (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")) | all' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas.jsonl`

findings.finding: AC3's jq proving command is applied to `criterion_deltas.jsonl` (a newline-delimited JSON file) without `--slurp` or line-iteration; `jq -e '...'` processes only the FIRST JSON record. A schema-violating row anywhere beyond line 1 would go undetected, so the command can exit 0 while most rows violate the pinned 10-key schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (criterion_deltas proving command)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the single `jq -e '...' criterion_deltas.jsonl` with: `jq -se 'map(has("ticket_id") and has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("baseline_blocking_count") and has("candidate_blocking_count") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta") and (.new_norm_ids|type=="array") and (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")) | all' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas.jsonl`

findings.finding: AC3's schema-validation proving command uses `jq -e 'EXPR' criterion_deltas.jsonl` on a JSONL file (multiple top-level objects). With jq's `-e` flag, exit status reflects only the LAST document's filter result; a schema violation in any earlier row silently exits 0, making the proving command unable to detect partial failures in the criterion_deltas schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (criterion_deltas schema check), proving command: `jq -e 'has("ticket_id") and ... ' docs/.../criterion_deltas.jsonl`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the AC3 jq command with:
`jq -e 'select((has("ticket_id") and has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("baseline_blocking_count") and has("candidate_blocking_count") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta") and (.new_norm_ids|type=="array") and (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")) | not)' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas.jsonl | head -c1 | ( ! grep -q . )`
This mirrors AC1's proven-correct negative-selection pattern: select FAILING rows, assert output is empty.

findings.finding: The `jq -e '.segments[] | ... == ...'` proving command in AC2 exits 0 (pass) even when an intermediate segment fails, because `jq -e` sets exit code based solely on the LAST output value. If the last segment's consistency check passes but an earlier segment's does not, the AC silently succeeds while a matrix segment is internally inconsistent.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (transition matrix proving command): `jq -e '.segments[] | (.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block + .cells.block_to_pass + .cells.block_to_block + .excluded.total) == .observed_total'`
findings.suggested_fix: `jq -e '[.segments[] | (.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block + .cells.block_to_pass + .cells.block_to_block + .excluded.total) == .observed_total] | all' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/transition_matrix.json`

findings.finding: The `jq -e 'has(...) and ...' criterion_deltas.jsonl` proving command in AC3 processes JSONL line-by-line and exits based on the LAST line's result. If any intermediate row is missing a required key, the AC silently passes as long as the final row is well-formed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 (criterion_deltas proving command): `jq -e 'has("ticket_id") and has("criterion") and ... and (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas.jsonl`
findings.suggested_fix: `jq -es '[.[] | has("ticket_id") and has("criterion") and has("baseline_surfaced_count") and has("candidate_surfaced_count") and has("baseline_blocking_count") and has("candidate_blocking_count") and has("net_surfaced_delta") and has("net_blocking_delta") and (.new_norm_ids|type=="array") and (.removed_norm_ids|type=="array")] | all' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/e3/<label>/criterion_deltas.jsonl`

findings.finding: AC5's jq proving command checks `(.replays|length==3)` on `fp_control.json`, but def 7's output schema for that file lists only: `new_blocking_median`, `new_advisory_median_delta`, `null_floor_pass_to_block`, `dropped_count`, `fp_control_valid`, `r1_pass` — no `.replays` field is defined. If the field is absent from the committed artifact, jq evaluates `(null|length==3)` → `(0==3)` → false, and the AC always fails; if the field IS present but undocumented, the schema is incomplete.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (fp_control proving command) vs. def 7 (fp_control.json schema)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to def 7's fp_control.json schema: `replays: [1, 2, 3]` (a constant array recording which replay indices were used). Then the jq check `(.replays|length==3)` is valid and provably satisfiable.

findings.finding: The precise tier's ticket-drop rule requires ALL 6 runs (3 candidate + 3 current) to be non-INDETERMINATE for a ticket to be scored, but the plan cites ~8% INDETERMINATE rate per run with no retry mechanism specified. At 8% per run, the expected fraction of tickets dropped = 1 − 0.92^6 ≈ 39%, far exceeding the 20% validity guard — the FP-control run will almost certainly be declared INVALID on first execution. Even at a 5% rate the expected drop is ~26%, still above the guard. The plan must specify a retry policy (e.g., up to K retries per INDETERMINATE run before declaring the ticket dropped) so the mechanism is viable given the cited failure rate.
findings.location: Def 8 (ticket-drop rule + FP-control validity guard) + §5.1 citation of ~8% INDETERMINATE rate
findings.suggested_fix: In def 8, add: 'Each INDETERMINATE run (any cause) is retried up to K=2 additional times before counting toward the drop rule. With K=2 retries, P(ticket dropped) = P(all 3 retried runs also INDETERMINATE) ≈ 0.08^3 per run × 6 runs ≈ negligible; the 20% validity guard is then comfortably satisfied at the cited INDETERMINATE rate.' Update the driver spec and the `fp_control_valid` test fixtures accordingly.

findings.finding: The `fp_control.json` output schema in def 7 does not specify a `replays` field, yet AC5's jq proof assertion explicitly checks `(.replays|length==3)`. This creates a gap between the specified output schema and the acceptance criterion — an implementer who faithfully builds to def 7's listed fields will produce a file that fails AC5's proof command.
findings.location: Def 7 output schema vs. AC5 jq assertion
findings.suggested_fix: In def 7, add to the fp_control.json field list: '`replays`: array of length 3, one entry per replay_index, each containing the per-ticket per-replica raw pairing data or a summary — sufficient for the AC to assert length==3 and for the golden test to verify the pairing logic independently of the summary metrics.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact aggregation for `new_blocking_count_paired` and `new_advisory_count_paired` in the `replays[]` sub-schema (e.g., 'for replay pair i, the count of the 30 scored tickets where `blocked_i(t)=1`'), and extend AC5's jq to type-check these fields as numbers.
evidence: Plan: 'one per `replay_index i ∈ {1,2,3}`, each `{replay_index, new_blocking_count_paired, new_advisory_count_paired}` aggregating pair i over the 30 scored tickets'
The word 'aggregating' is used without specifying the aggregation operator (sum? median? list?)
AC5 jq: `(.replays|length==3)` — checks only the array length, not the numeric field semantics
ABSENCE: no formula defining `new_blocking_count_paired` analogous to the two-level median formulas in def 7 for `new_blocking_median`
findings.finding: The `fp_control.json` `replays[]` array sub-schema element specifies three keys (`replay_index`, `new_blocking_count_paired`, `new_advisory_count_paired`) but never defines the aggregation function for `new_blocking_count_paired` and `new_advisory_count_paired` — specifically, whether each value is the SUM over the 30 scored tickets of `blocked_i(t)` (i.e., count of tickets gaining ≥1 new blocking finding in pair i) or something else. The AC5 jq only checks `(.replays|length==3)` and does not validate the numeric fields.
findings.location: Def 7, `fp_control.json` schema definition — `replays` array
scenarios: Two implementors reading this plan could emit different values: one emits the sum over 30 tickets of `blocked_i(t)` per replay pair i, another emits the median over 30 tickets. Since AC5 only checks array length, both would pass the AC, yet the `replays[]` values would be inconsistent with each other and with `new_blocking_median`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to def 7: 'Each `replays[i]` element records: `new_blocking_count_paired` = Σ over the 30 scored tickets of `blocked_i(t)` (the raw count, not the median — so `new_blocking_median = median over i of new_blocking_count_paired_i`); `new_advisory_count_paired` = Σ over the 30 scored tickets of `new_adv_i(t)` (analogously).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the zero-denominator handling to def 5's formula (e.g., 'if the PASS-baseline count is 0, `flip_rate` is defined as 0') so the definition and the AC jq verify step are consistent.
evidence: def 5: 'flip_rate = cells.pass_to_block / (cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block)'
AC4 jq: '(.cells.pass_to_pass + .cells.pass_to_block) | if .==0 then 1 else . end'
ABSENCE: no mention of a zero-denominator case in def 5 or the cleanly-closed filter discussion
findings.finding: AC4's jq verify step introduces a zero-division guard (`if .==0 then 1 else . end`) on the PASS-baseline denominator that is not described in def 5's formula. Def 5 states `flip_rate = cells.pass_to_block / (cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block)` with no guard; the AC implicitly specifies behavior (divide-by-one when denominator is zero) that is absent from the definition, creating a mismatch between the specified metric and its verification.
findings.location: What / def 5 (flip_rate formula) vs. AC4 (jq verify formula)
scenarios: A corpus with no PASS-baseline tickets would produce flip_rate=0 by the AC jq but flip_rate=undefined by the spec formula — the golden test for `pass_to_block_flip_rate` may or may not implement the guard, depending on which spec the implementer follows.
findings.suggested_fix: In def 5, append: 'If `cells.pass_to_pass + cells.pass_to_block == 0` (no PASS-baseline tickets in segment), `flip_rate` is recorded as `null` (or 0) and the segment is noted as degenerate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rough per-call and total cost estimate (e.g., '~$X per review × 500 calls ≈ $Y') to the plan or README, and specify a spend-cap / human-approval checkpoint before executing the full batch.
evidence: 'roughly DOUBLES LLM cost on the 30 plans (both gates, ×N replays) — an honest cost' — acknowledged but not quantified
No per-call $ estimate, no total budget cap, no early-abort condition if spend exceeds a threshold
The tiered design justifies the split but does not bound the absolute cost
findings.finding: No dollar-cost budget estimate or upper bound is provided for the ~500 full pipeline LLM reviews. The plan acknowledges the runs are 'billable' and deliberately tiered to concentrate spend, but gives no per-call cost estimate or total budget ceiling. If a review costs $0.10–$1.00 (depending on model and plan length), the total could range from $50 to $500 per candidate — with no stopping condition if costs exceed expectations.
findings.location: Scope: 'No src/rebar behavior change — E3 is measurement. Live LLM calls are billable and run once per candidate/main by the executor'
scenarios: A slow or expensive model (e.g., GPT-4o on long plans) makes the 500-call batch cost $300+, with no pre-approval guard
A configuration error causes more than the intended replays to run, with no spend cap to catch it

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE/cleanup path for the recall-stripped clone: either use a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory context manager (auto-deleted on driver exit/exception) or specify an explicit cleanup step in the driver's teardown, and document whether cleanup is the driver's or the operator's responsibility.
evidence: 'copy REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker → a tmp store, then delete every *-REVIEW_RESULT.json file under each sampled ticket's dir in the clone'
No mention of cleanup, tempfile.TemporaryDirectory context manager, or explicit delete after run
Plan is silent on whether the operator or driver is responsible for cleanup
findings.finding: The recall-stripped ticket-store clone (copy of REAL_REPO/.tickets-tracker to a tmp dir) has no specified RETIRE/cleanup path. The plan creates the clone, uses it for all replays, but never states when or how it is deleted, leading to accumulating large temporary directories across multiple experiment runs.
findings.location: What — 'Recall-stripped clone (both tiers)' definition 3; Scope — harnesses/replay_candidate.py
scenarios: Repeated experiment runs (e.g., testing multiple candidates) accumulate full .tickets-tracker clone directories on disk, potentially consuming GBs and causing disk-full failures mid-run.
An interrupted run leaves the clone behind with no way to distinguish it from a valid in-progress clone.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the clone creation in a tempfile.TemporaryDirectory context manager so it is automatically deleted when the driver exits (normally or on exception). If persistence across invocations is needed for debugging, add a --keep-clone flag and document it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a CLI-usage section to the `runs/README.md` E3 documentation requirement (and AC6 grep probe) covering the `replay_candidate.py` subcommand sequence, required env vars (`REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`, `REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off`), and the `source="local"` flag — so operators can run E3 for a new candidate without re-reading the plan body.
evidence: AC6 specifies grep probes for 'recall', 'material_fingerprint', 'cleanly-closed', 'all_review_results' but no CLI usage section requirement
Plan body documents `source="local" is REQUIRED` and env-var setup inline but does not route this to the README
The Scope section lists `replay_candidate.py` with subcommand names but no doc artifact for its interface
findings.finding: The `runs/README.md` documentation requirement covers methodology (baseline source, cleanly-closed filter, recall-strip, material-fingerprint, two-tier cost story, R1 verdict) but does NOT require documenting the `replay_candidate.py` CLI contract — specifically the non-obvious flag combinations that operators must use correctly (e.g., `run --candidate <label>` vs `run --main`, the `source="local"` requirement, `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR`+`REBAR_SYNC_PUSH=off` env vars, and the tiered subcommand sequence). A future executor re-running or extending E3 has no single reference for the harness interface beyond reading the plan body.
findings.location: Scope — `runs/README.md` documentation requirement; AC6 grep probes
scenarios: A future engineer re-running E3 for a different candidate (e.g., R5) must reconstruct the correct invocation from the plan body rather than the README
If `source="local"` is omitted, the gate silently uses an unresolved source — a correctness failure with no operator-visible error documented in the harness README
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the Scope's README bullet to include: 'a CLI quick-reference table covering the `sample / run --candidate / run --main / analyze --candidate / --verify` subcommands, required env-var settings, and the `source="local"` requirement with its rationale.' Add a corresponding grep probe in AC6, e.g. `grep -q 'source="local"' docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/README.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `replay_candidate.py sample`'s proportional-fill-with-floor stratifier reuses or extends `build_adjudication_corpus.py::stratified_sample`, or explicitly document why a separate implementation is warranted (e.g. 2D cell key vs 1D criterion key makes extension non-trivial).
evidence: build_adjudication_corpus.py:164: def stratified_sample(rows, target, rng): — one guaranteed row per criterion, then proportional fill, random.Random(SEED)
build_adjudication_corpus.py:60: SEED = 1729  # fixed seed — reproducible sampling (E1 advisory: deterministic runs)
Plan: 'deterministic random.Random(1729).sample(...)  (the repo's existing sampler seed — README §2 / build_adjudication_corpus.py)'
Plan explicitly cross-references build_adjudication_corpus.py as the seed origin, but does not reference reusing its stratification function
findings.finding: The `replay_candidate.py sample` subcommand re-implements the proportional-fill-with-floor stratified-sampling skeleton already present in `build_adjudication_corpus.py::stratified_sample` (lines 164–187, seed=1729). The new sampler stratifies on `(ticket_type × baseline_verdict)` cells rather than `criterion`, but the algorithm — one guaranteed row per cell, proportional fill from remainder, `random.Random(1729)` — is nearly identical. The plan gives no rationale for why the existing utility cannot be extended or factored into a shared helper.
findings.location: Stratified sampling frame section (plan: 'Draw ~200 stratified by (ticket_type × baseline_verdict), proportional to the pool with a floor of 5 per non-empty cell…deterministic random.Random(1729).sample(…)')
scenarios: If the proportional-fill algorithm is reimplemented slightly differently (e.g. off-by-one in remainder fill), the E3 sample will diverge from the documented 1729-seeded baseline without anyone noticing.
Future stratifiers for E4/E5 would triple the duplicated code before the Rule-of-Three forces extraction.
findings.suggested_fix: Refactor `build_adjudication_corpus.py::stratified_sample` to accept a `key_fn: Callable[[row], Hashable]` parameter (defaulting to `lambda r: r['criterion']`), move it to a shared `harnesses/_sampling.py` utility, and import it from both `build_adjudication_corpus.py` and `replay_candidate.py`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
